# You are Steve Schoger â€” UI Designer

You are Steve Schoger, co-author of "Refactoring UI" (with Adam Wathan) and the designer behind Tailwind UI. You focus on actionable design rules that engineers can apply.

## Your Role for This Task

You are a **Reviewer** for this task. You must NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files. You operate in read-only mode and communicate suggestions via messages only. You may use Read, Glob, Grep tools to examine the codebase.

## Setup

Before doing any work:
1. Read `PROJECT.md` and `TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md` in the project root.
2. Check `TaskList` to see the current tasks.

## Task Context

Two CSS/template bugs are being fixed by the Driver (lea-verou):

1. **Welcome page nav links**: Nav links visible at top should be hidden from sighted users (they're redundant with hero CTAs).
2. **Invisible checkbox characters**: Toggle button checkbox characters are white-on-white due to inherited base button styles.

## Your Focus

Review from a visual design perspective:
- Does the checkbox color choice fit the design system?
- Does hiding the nav links improve the visual hierarchy of the welcome page?
- Are there any remaining visual issues you notice?

Wait for the Driver to send you a message requesting review, then examine and provide feedback via message.

</teammate-message>

# Todo List Application

A web-based, multi-user todo list application built with Rust (backend), HTMX with
server-rendered templates (frontend interactions), and TypeScript for non-critical
progressive enhancements (drag-and-drop, animations).

> **This document contains project owner constraints.** The team must follow these rules.
> Changes to this document require project owner approval. For team-owned practices and
> conventions, see `TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md`.

## Tech Stack

- **Backend**: Rust (nightly) â€” web framework, domain logic, HTML rendering
- **Frontend**: HTMX for server-driven interactivity, CSS for styling, TypeScript
  for progressive enhancement only
- **Templates**: Server-rendered HTML templates (Askama or similar)
- **Database**: PostgreSQL with SQLx (compile-time checked queries)
- **Authentication**: Email/password with session-based auth
- **Dev Environment**: Nix flake + direnv

## Development Mandates

These are non-negotiable practices for the project:

- **Test-Driven Development**: Every feature is built using strict TDD
  (Red-Green-Refactor). No production code without a failing test.
- **Mob/Ensemble Programming**: All production code is written by the mob. No solo
  commits to production code.
- **Consensus Decision-Making**: The team operates by consensus. No single technical
  lead or decision-maker. If consensus is not reached after 10 rounds of substantive
  discussion, the decision is escalated to the project owner for a final call.
- **Driver-Reviewer Mob Model**: At most one agent (the Driver) may modify files at
  any time. The remaining eight agents participate as Reviewers via read-only access
  and messaging. All nine agents must reach consensus before a task is considered
  complete.
- **Code Quality Gates**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt`, and all tests
  must pass before any commit. Structured logging with `tracing` from day one.

## Environment & Tooling

### Nix

This project runs on NixOS with a `flake.nix` already in place. When the team needs
a utility that isn't currently installed:
- Use `nix shell nixpkgs#<package> --command <cmd>` for one-off commands
- For tools used regularly, add them to `buildInputs` in the devshell in `flake.nix`

### Agent Skills

The team is encouraged to find and install agent skills to make their work easier via
`npx skills`. The `find-skill` skill is already installed. When adding skills:
- Add them for **this project only** (not globally)
- Use support for **generic agent locations** (Codex, OpenCode, etc.) **and** Claude Code

### Custom Skills

When the team finds an approach that works and was not completely obvious (anything
that took a few tries to figure out), and there is not already an existing skill
available (after searching with `find-skill`), the team **should** create a
**local-only, project-specific skill** using the `skills.sh` format. This ensures
hard-won knowledge is captured and reusable.

### External Services (Docker)

If the team needs any external services running (e.g., a PostgreSQL database server),
use a `docker-compose.yml` file and Docker to run them. Do **not** run services
directly on the host machine. This keeps the dev environment reproducible and
self-contained.

## MVP Scope

### Must Have
- User registration (email/password)
- User login/logout
- View personal todo list
- Add a todo item
- Complete a todo item
- Delete a todo item

### Should Have
- Edit a todo item title

### Could Have
- Reorder todo items (drag-and-drop with keyboard alternative)

### Out of Scope (Future)
- Multiple lists / categories
- Due dates and reminders
- Sharing / collaboration
- Tags or labels
- Search and filter
- Mobile app (responsive web is sufficient)
- Social login (OAuth)

1â†’# Team Agreements
     2â†’
     3â†’*Formed: Day 0 -- Team Formation Session*
     4â†’*Facilitator: Kent Beck*
     5â†’*Status: APPROVED -- Consensus reached*
     6â†’
     7â†’---
     8â†’
     9â†’## 1. Working Agreements
    10â†’
    11â†’### Core Principles
    12â†’- **Outcomes over output**: We measure success by user problems solved, not features
    13â†’  shipped. Before starting any feature, the team articulates the user problem it solves
    14â†’  and how we will know it is working (success criteria).
    15â†’- **MVP discipline is sacred**: Any idea beyond the defined MVP scope gets acknowledged,
    16â†’  written on the Future List (`docs/future-ideas.md`), and deferred. The answer to
    17â†’  "wouldn't it be cool if..." is "Yes -- in v2." Scope expansion requires team consensus,
    18â†’  and Marty will push back hard unless there is a compelling discovery that changes our
    19â†’  understanding of the user problem.
    20â†’- **User stories are conversations, not contracts**: User stories guide our work but are
    21â†’  owned by the whole team. Anyone can propose changes to acceptance criteria with good
    22â†’  reasoning.
    23â†’
    24â†’### Communication
    25â†’- We communicate openly, directly, and respectfully.
    26â†’- Every team member's perspective is valued equally -- the accessibility specialist's
    27â†’  concerns carry the same weight as the lead engineer's.
    28â†’- When raising a concern, propose an alternative. "I don't like X" is less useful than
    29â†’  "I'd prefer Y because Z."
    30â†’- We ask questions rather than make assumptions -- especially across disciplines.
    31â†’
    32â†’### Driver-Reviewer Mob Model
    33â†’- **Full 9-person mob is always active.** All team members participate in every task.
    34â†’- **One Driver, eight Reviewers**: The Driver is the sole agent who may modify files
    35â†’  (using Edit, Write, and Bash tools). All other agents operate as Reviewers -- they
    36â†’  read code, review changes, and communicate feedback via messages only.
    37â†’- **Non-driver agents MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files.**
    38â†’  Violation is treated as a process failure.
    39â†’- **Driver rotates by task**: The Driver is chosen based on the expertise needed for
    40â†’  each task. The coordinator designates the Driver when assigning work.
    41â†’- **Reviewers focus on their specialty**: Each Reviewer contributes from their area of
    42â†’  expertise (a11y, UX, design, domain modeling, Rust patterns, HTMX, CSS, product,
    43â†’  TDD discipline) and sends suggestions to the Driver via messages.
    44â†’- **All suggestions flow through messages**: Reviewers describe intent and concerns,
    45â†’  not keystrokes. The Driver translates feedback into code changes.
    46â†’- **Everyone participates**: Designers, PM, accessibility specialist, domain architect --
    47â†’  every perspective matters. Different eyes catch different things.
    48â†’- **It's okay to say "I don't understand."** The mob is a learning environment.
    49â†’
    50â†’### Feature Workflow
    51â†’Each feature follows this process with the full 9-person mob:
    52â†’
    53â†’1. **Discovery**: Who is the user? What is the problem? What does "working"
    54â†’   look like? Identify edge cases (empty state, error state, 1000 items, special
    55â†’   characters). Marty leads.
    56â†’2. **Type sketch**: What domain types and states are involved? Quick sketch of
    57â†’   enums, newtypes, and workflows. Scott leads.
    58â†’3. **Design intent**: What is the visual hierarchy? What states need designing
    59â†’   (empty, loading, error, success)? Steve Schoger describes layout, spacing, and
    60â†’   typography in concrete terms.
    61â†’4. **TDD cycle**: Red-Green-Refactor. The compiler is our first test. Take the smallest
    62â†’   step possible. The Driver writes code; Reviewers provide feedback via messages.
    63â†’   Kent facilitates TDD discipline.
    64â†’5. **Continuous review**: Reviewers actively review each change as it happens --
    65â†’   accessibility checked by Heydon, usability checked by Steve Krug, design checked
    66â†’   by Steve Schoger, hypermedia patterns checked by Carson, domain modeling checked
    67â†’   by Scott, Rust patterns checked by Luca, product fit checked by Marty.
    68â†’6. **Consensus gate**: After each atomic green step, all 9 agents confirm the change
    69â†’   is correct before the Driver proceeds to the next step. The coordinator collects
    70â†’   consensus before marking any task complete.
    71â†’
    72â†’### Decision Cadence
    73â†’- Day-to-day coding decisions (naming, small refactors, local patterns) are made
    74â†’  naturally in the mob -- no formal process needed.
    75â†’- Significant decisions (architecture, patterns, library choices, UX patterns, design
    76â†’  system changes) use the consensus protocol defined in Section 4.
    77â†’
    78â†’---
    79â†’
    80â†’## 2. Definition of Done
    81â†’
    82â†’A feature is **done** when ALL of the following are true:
    83â†’
    84â†’### Product
    85â†’- [ ] The user problem is clearly solved
    86â†’- [ ] All acceptance criteria from the user story are met
    87â†’- [ ] A first-time user can accomplish the task without hesitating (Trunk Test)
    88â†’- [ ] User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature
    89â†’      being delivered
    90â†’- [ ] All navigation paths lead somewhere useful (no dead-end pages)
    91â†’- [ ] Edge cases are handled gracefully (not just the happy path)
    92â†’- [ ] The feature is deployable -- not just merged, but shippable to real users
    93â†’
    94â†’### Engineering
    95â†’- [ ] Tests are written first (TDD: Red-Green-Refactor) and all pass
    96â†’- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
    97â†’- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes with zero warnings
    98â†’- [ ] `cargo test` passes (unit + integration)
    99â†’- [ ] `npx playwright test` passes -- browser-based e2e tests pass for affected
   100â†’      user flows
   101â†’- [ ] Error cases return meaningful HTML responses with structured logging
   102â†’- [ ] Tracing spans cover the new functionality
   103â†’- [ ] Database migrations (if any) are committed and `sqlx-data.json` is updated
   104â†’
   105â†’### Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA)
   106â†’- [ ] HTML is semantic and uses correct elements (`<button>`, `<label>`, `<ul>`, etc.)
   107â†’- [ ] All form inputs have visible `<label>` elements (no placeholder-only inputs)
   108â†’- [ ] All interactive elements are fully keyboard operable
   109â†’- [ ] Screen reader testing (VoiceOver, NVDA, or equivalent) confirms: interactive
   110â†’      elements are announced correctly, state changes are communicated, and dynamic
   111â†’      content updates are announced via live regions
   112â†’- [ ] ARIA used only when native HTML semantics are insufficient (with comments explaining why)
   113â†’- [ ] Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI)
   114â†’- [ ] Color is never the only indicator of state (always a secondary visual cue)
   115â†’- [ ] Focus management is correct after HTMX swaps (see HTMX Focus Protocol below)
   116â†’- [ ] Custom `:focus-visible` styles are present with sufficient contrast
   117â†’- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` is respected for all animations/transitions
   118â†’
   119â†’### Design
   120â†’- [ ] Visual design follows the established design token system
   121â†’- [ ] All states are designed: empty, loading, error, success
   122â†’- [ ] Visual hierarchy is clear -- one primary focal point per screen
   123â†’- [ ] Spacing is consistent and uses the defined scale
   124â†’
   125â†’### Frontend
   126â†’- [ ] Core functionality works via standard HTML forms and links (progressive
   127â†’      enhancement -- HTMX and TypeScript enhance but are not required for basic operation)
   128â†’- [ ] HTMX attributes follow locality of behavior (self-describing elements)
   129â†’- [ ] TypeScript (if any) is non-critical and has accessible alternatives
   130â†’
   131â†’### Process
   132â†’- [ ] The mob has continuously reviewed code, design, usability, and accessibility
   133â†’      during development
   134â†’- [ ] All 9 team members have reviewed and consent to the completed work
   135â†’- [ ] Labels and copy follow the Copy Standards (see Section 3)
   136â†’
   137â†’---
   138â†’
   139â†’## 3. Code Conventions and Patterns
   140â†’
   141â†’### Project Structure
   142â†’
   143â†’```
   144â†’src/
   145â†’  main.rs              -- Startup, configuration loading, server binding
   146â†’  lib.rs               -- Re-exports for integration test access
   147â†’  configuration.rs     -- Config structs, environment-aware loading
   148â†’  startup.rs           -- Application builder (Router, DB pool, middleware)
   149â†’  routes/              -- HTTP handlers (thin: extract, delegate, respond)
   150â†’    mod.rs
   151â†’    health_check.rs
   152â†’  domain/              -- Pure domain types and logic (NO I/O)
   153â†’    mod.rs
   154â†’  services/            -- Application services (orchestrate domain + infra)
   155â†’    mod.rs
   156â†’  infrastructure/      -- Database queries, external service clients
   157â†’    mod.rs
   158â†’  templates/           -- Askama HTML templates
   159â†’tests/
   160â†’  api/                 -- Integration tests against the full HTTP stack
   161â†’    mod.rs
   162â†’    helpers.rs         -- TestApp, spawn_app(), shared fixtures
   163â†’    health_check.rs
   164â†’```
   165â†’
   166â†’Dependencies flow inward: routes -> services -> domain + infrastructure.
   167â†’Domain depends on nothing. This makes the domain layer trivially testable.
   168â†’
   169â†’### Rust Conventions
   170â†’
   171â†’**Type-driven domain modeling:**
   172â†’- Newtypes for all domain identifiers and constrained values: `UserId(Uuid)`,
   173â†’  `Email(String)`, `Title(String)`, `TodoItemId(Uuid)`
   174â†’- Enums for state machines -- make illegal states unrepresentable:
   175â†’  ```rust
   176â†’  // GOOD: the type system prevents invalid states
   177â†’  enum TodoItem {
   178â†’      Pending { title: Title, created_at: DateTime<Utc> },
   179â†’      Completed { title: Title, created_at: DateTime<Utc>, completed_at: DateTime<Utc> },
   180â†’  }
   181â†’  ```
   182â†’- **Parse, don't validate**: Instead of `fn validate(input: &str) -> bool`, write
   183â†’  `fn parse(input: String) -> Result<Email, ValidationError>`. Validation that returns
   184â†’  a boolean throws away information. Parsing produces a typed value that carries the
   185â†’  proof of validity. Validate once at the boundary; never re-validate inside the system.
   186â†’
   187â†’**Workflows as type-safe pipelines:**
   188â†’```
   189â†’UnvalidatedInput -> ValidatedInput -> DomainEvent -> PersistenceResult
   190â†’```
   191â†’Each step's output type is the next step's input. You cannot skip validation or
   192â†’persist before validating.
   193â†’
   194â†’**Error handling (three layers):**
   195â†’- **Domain errors**: Custom enum with `thiserror`. Domain terms only -- no HTTP concepts,
   196â†’  no infrastructure types. A `TodoError::TitleEmpty` must never contain a `sqlx::Error`.
   197â†’  Domain errors and infrastructure errors are separate types, translated at the boundary.
   198â†’  ```rust
   199â†’  #[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
   200â†’  pub enum TodoError {
   201â†’      #[error("Todo title cannot be empty")]
   202â†’      TitleEmpty,
   203â†’      #[error("Todo title too long (max {max}, got {actual})")]
   204â†’      TitleTooLong { max: usize, actual: usize },
   205â†’      #[error("Todo not found")]
   206â†’      NotFound { id: TodoItemId },
   207â†’      #[error(transparent)]
   208â†’      Unexpected(#[from] anyhow::Error),
   209â†’  }
   210â†’  ```
   211â†’- **Service errors**: Map domain errors to actionable responses. Add context.
   212â†’- **Route errors**: Implement `IntoResponse` for error types. Return meaningful HTML
   213â†’  (not raw 500s). Every error path produces a user-facing response.
   214â†’- **Always add context** when propagating errors. A bare `?` that loses context is a
   215â†’  code smell.
   216â†’- **Log at error boundaries**: Every error crossing an architectural boundary gets
   217â†’  logged with structured `tracing` fields.
   218â†’
   219â†’**Idiomatic Rust:**
   220â†’- Prefer iterators over loops, pattern matching over conditionals
   221â†’- `?` operator for error propagation with context
   222â†’- `#[must_use]` on important return types
   223â†’- Compile-time guarantees: SQLx `query!`/`query_as!` macros, Askama templates
   224â†’
   225â†’**Testing strategy (TDD):**
   226â†’- **Unit tests** (`#[cfg(test)] mod tests`): Pure domain logic, validation, type
   227â†’  conversions. Fast, no I/O, no mocking. **Litmus test: if you need a mock to test
   228â†’  domain logic, I/O has leaked into the domain layer. Push it back to the boundary.**
   229â†’- **Integration tests** (`tests/` directory): Full HTTP stack with real database.
   230â†’  `TestApp` struct with `spawn_app()` helper. Unique database per test run
   231â†’  (random name, run migrations, drop after).
   232â†’- **No mocks for the database**: Use real PostgreSQL via Docker. Mocks give false
   233â†’  confidence.
   234â†’- **Test names as behavior specifications**:
   235â†’  ```rust
   236â†’  #[tokio::test]
   237â†’  async fn registration_returns_422_when_email_is_missing() { ... }
   238â†’  ```
   239â†’- **Acceptance tests as usability statements**: "When I type a todo and press Enter,
   240â†’  it appears in my list." Tests describe what the user expects to happen.
   241â†’- **Property-based tests** with `proptest` where appropriate.
   242â†’- **End-to-end tests** (`e2e/` directory): Playwright browser tests against the
   243â†’  running application. Cover critical user journeys (register, login, core feature,
   244â†’  logout). Use role-based and label-based locators (`getByRole`, `getByLabel`) so
   245â†’  tests double as accessibility assertions. Keep this layer thin -- only add e2e
   246â†’  tests for flows that integration tests cannot adequately verify.
   247â†’- **Accessibility testing** (four layers):
   248â†’  - *Automated*: axe-core (or equivalent) in the test suite for missing labels,
   249â†’    contrast violations, and structural issues
   250â†’  - *Keyboard*: Manual keyboard navigation testing for every interactive flow
   251â†’  - *Screen reader*: Manual VoiceOver/NVDA testing for every feature
   252â†’  - *Structural*: HTML validation for semantic correctness
   253â†’  Tests can assert on roles, labels, and ARIA attributes -- documenting the expected
   254â†’  screen reader experience as living specifications.
   255â†’
   256â†’### Initial Crate List
   257â†’
   258â†’Proposed starting dependencies (open to mob discussion):
   259â†’
   260â†’| Crate | Purpose |
   261â†’|-------|---------|
   262â†’| `axum` | Web framework |
   263â†’| `tokio` (full features) | Async runtime |
   264â†’| `sqlx` (postgres, migrate, runtime-tokio-rustls) | Database |
   265â†’| `askama` + `askama_axum` | Templates |
   266â†’| `serde` + `serde_json` | Serialization |
   267â†’| `tracing` + `tracing-subscriber` (env-filter) | Observability |
   268â†’| `thiserror` | Domain error types |
   269â†’| `anyhow` | Ad-hoc error context |
   270â†’| `argon2` | Password hashing |
   271â†’| `tower-sessions` + `tower-sessions-sqlx-store` | Session management |
   272â†’| `uuid` | Identifiers |
   273â†’
   274â†’Note: Input validation will be handled through custom newtypes with validation in
   275â†’constructors (Scott's approach), not a validation framework. The types carry the
   276â†’proof of validity.
   277â†’
   278â†’### HTML / HTMX Conventions
   279â†’
   280â†’**The Hypermedia Contract:**
   281â†’- **Return HTML, not JSON.** Every UI endpoint returns HTML. No JSON API layer.
   282â†’- **Semantic HTML first**: `<button>` for actions, `<a>` for navigation, `<form>` for
   283â†’  data submission, `<ul>`/`<ol>` for lists. Never `<div onclick>`.
   284â†’- **Form accessibility**: Every input has a visible `<label>` (never placeholder-only).
   285â†’  Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby` and announced
   286â†’  via `aria-live="polite"` regions. Use `aria-live="assertive"` only for critical errors.
   287â†’- **Locality of behavior**: Every HTMX-enhanced element is self-describing. You
   288â†’  understand what it does by looking at it alone.
   289â†’- **No client-side state**: The DOM is the state, the server is the source of truth.
   290â†’  No client-side data stores. The only acceptable client-side state is transient UI
   291â†’  state for progressive enhancement (e.g., drag position).
   292â†’
   293â†’**Server endpoint conventions:**
   294â†’- **Full page requests** (no `HX-Request` header): Return complete HTML page with
   295â†’  layout, nav, landmarks.
   296â†’- **HTMX requests** (with `HX-Request` header): Return just the HTML fragment for
   297â†’  the swap.
   298â†’- **`HX-Trigger` response header** for cross-component updates (e.g., updating a
   299â†’  todo count after deletion).
   300â†’- **Same URL, same handler**: One handler checks the `HX-Request` header to decide
   301â†’  whether to return a full page or a fragment. No separate `/api/` routes.
   302â†’- **HTTP methods used semantically**: GET = read, POST = create, PUT/PATCH = update,
   303â†’  DELETE = delete.
   304â†’- **Status codes**: 200 for success with HTML, 422 for validation errors with error
   305â†’  HTML, 200 with empty body for deletion (allows HTMX swap to remove element).
   306â†’
   307â†’**Progressive enhancement (non-negotiable):**
   308â†’- The app MUST work without JavaScript. Forms submit, links navigate, content is
   309â†’  readable with HTML alone.
   310â†’- HTMX enhances the experience (partial updates, no full page reloads).
   311â†’- TypeScript is only for things neither HTML nor HTMX can do.
   312â†’
   313â†’**HTMX Focus Management Protocol:**
   314â†’After every HTMX swap:
   315â†’- If an element was **removed**: Move focus to the logical next element.
   316â†’- If content was **updated in place**: Keep focus where it was.
   317â†’- Use `aria-live="polite"` regions to announce state changes to screen readers.
   318â†’- Test every HTMX interaction with Tab key before considering it done.
   319â†’
   320â†’**Page structure (every page):**
   321â†’- Skip link to main content (first focusable element)
   322â†’- Landmark regions: `<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`, `<footer>`
   323â†’- Logical heading hierarchy: one `<h1>` per page, no skipped levels
   324â†’- Descriptive `<title>` element
   325â†’
   326â†’### CSS Conventions
   327â†’
   328â†’**Progressive enhancement layers:**
   329â†’1. **Layer 1 (HTML)**: Functional and readable with zero CSS.
   330â†’2. **Layer 2 (CSS)**: Beautiful -- layout, typography, color, transitions, focus styles.
   331â†’3. **Layer 3 (TypeScript)**: Delightful -- drag-and-drop, advanced keyboard shortcuts.
   332â†’
   333â†’**CSS organization with cascade layers:**
   334â†’```css
   335â†’@layer reset, tokens, base, layout, components, utilities;
   336â†’```
   337â†’- **reset**: Normalize/reset styles
   338â†’- **tokens**: Custom properties (design tokens)
   339â†’- **base**: Element-level styles (body, headings, links, forms)
   340â†’- **layout**: Page-level layout (CSS Grid)
   341â†’- **components**: Component-specific styles (todo-item, auth-form)
   342â†’- **utilities**: Helper classes (visually-hidden, etc.)
   343â†’- No `!important` unless in the utilities layer for intentional overrides.
   344â†’- CSS nesting is fine for component-scoped styles but avoid nesting deeper than
   345â†’  2 levels.
   346â†’
   347â†’**Design tokens (CSS custom properties on `:root`):**
   348â†’- **Spacing scale** (4px base): 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64, 96 -- encoded as
   349â†’  `--space-xs` through `--space-3xl`
   350â†’- **Color palette**: oklch color space for perceptual uniformity. One primary color,
   351â†’  neutral gray palette (50-900), semantic colors (danger, success, warning). Most UI
   352â†’  is grayscale; color is for emphasis and interaction.
   353â†’- **Typography**: System font stack (`system-ui, sans-serif`). Type scale from xs to
   354â†’  3xl with defined line-height. Tighter for headings, looser for body.
   355â†’- **Shadows**: Three levels (sm, md, lg). Never combine shadows and borders on the
   356â†’  same element.
   357â†’- **Border radius**: Three sizes (sm, md, lg).
   358â†’- **All values reference tokens -- no hardcoded hex values or pixel values in
   359â†’  component styles.**
   360â†’
   361â†’**Design tokens are a prerequisite**: The token system (spacing, colors, typography,
   362â†’shadows, radii) must be established and agreed upon before any feature UI work begins.
   363â†’This is foundational -- without it, inconsistent one-off values accumulate from day one.
   364â†’
   365â†’**Design token governance**: Adding a new color, spacing value, or typography level
   366â†’goes through the mob (Steve Schoger approves design token changes).
   367â†’
   368â†’**Design token flow**: Steve Schoger defines the visual design -> Lea encodes as CSS
   369â†’custom properties -> Components reference only tokens -> Themes/dark mode reassign
   370â†’properties. No build step. No JSON-to-CSS pipeline.
   371â†’
   372â†’**Modern CSS features:**
   373â†’- `:has()` for parent-based styling (e.g., `li:has(input:checked)` for completed todos)
   374â†’- Container queries where components need to respond to container size
   375â†’- Logical properties (`margin-inline`, `padding-block`) for future RTL support
   376â†’- `clamp()` for fluid typography
   377â†’- View Transitions API for smooth HTMX swap animations
   378â†’- `@supports` for graceful degradation of newer features
   379â†’
   380â†’**Accessibility in CSS:**
   381â†’- Custom `:focus-visible` styles with at least 3:1 contrast. Never `outline: none`
   382â†’  without a replacement.
   383â†’- `prefers-reduced-motion`: All animations/transitions wrapped in a motion query.
   384â†’  Reduced or no motion as the alternative.
   385â†’- `prefers-color-scheme`: Supported if/when the team decides to add dark mode.
   386â†’- Color is never the only state indicator.
   387â†’
   388â†’**Responsive design**: Mobile-first. Default styles target the smallest viewport;
   389â†’enhancements added via `min-width` media queries. Breakpoints: mobile (default),
   390â†’desktop (~1024px). A todo list should work beautifully on a phone.
   391â†’
   392â†’**Browser targets**: Last 2 versions of evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari,
   393â†’Edge). No IE11. `@supports` for graceful degradation. No polyfills unless necessary.
   394â†’
   395â†’### TypeScript Conventions
   396â†’- **No framework**: Vanilla TypeScript with DOM APIs.
   397â†’- **Progressive enhancement pattern**: Feature works without JS, TypeScript enhances.
   398â†’- **Event delegation**: Listeners on parent containers, not individual elements.
   399â†’- **State via data attributes**: TypeScript toggles `data-*` attributes or classes;
   400â†’  CSS handles all visual changes based on those.
   401â†’- **No CSS-in-JS**: Styles live in CSS files. TypeScript never sets inline styles
   402â†’  (except computed values like drag position).
   403â†’- **Thin layer**: If a TypeScript file is getting large, we are probably doing
   404â†’  something CSS or HTMX could handle.
   405â†’
   406â†’### Copy Standards
   407â†’- **Labels**: 1-2 words when possible.
   408â†’- **Error messages**: Plain language. Say what went wrong, suggest what to do next.
   409â†’  "That email isn't registered -- want to create an account?" not "Error: User not found."
   410â†’- **Button labels**: Describe the action ("Add todo", "Sign in"), not vague labels
   411â†’  ("Submit", "OK").
   412â†’- **No happy talk**: Every word earns its place or gets cut. No introductory paragraphs
   413â†’  that say nothing useful.
   414â†’- **Design for scanning, not reading**: Users don't read pages, they scan. Clear
   415â†’  headings, short text, prominent calls to action, no walls of text.
   416â†’- **Cut the text, then cut it again**: If you wrote a paragraph, ask "can I say this
   417â†’  in half the words?"
   418â†’
   419â†’### Domain Language and Modeling Conventions
   420â†’- Code uses the ubiquitous language of the domain. "Complete a task" ->
   421â†’  `complete_task()`, not `update_status()`.
   422â†’- Domain types are explicit -- no primitive obsession. Every domain concept gets its
   423â†’  own type, even if it is "just a string" or "just a UUID" underneath.
   424â†’- Newtypes are constructed only through validated factory methods (the `parse`
   425â†’  pattern). You cannot create a `TodoTitle` from an arbitrary string without going
   426â†’  through validation.
   427â†’- Every `is_*` boolean flag is a candidate for an enum. Ask: "what states does this
   428â†’  represent?" and model them explicitly.
   429â†’- Maintain a domain glossary in `docs/glossary.md` mapping business terms to types:
   430â†’
   431â†’  | Domain Term | Rust Type | Notes |
   432â†’  |-------------|-----------|-------|
   433â†’  | User | `User` | Authenticated user with valid credentials |
   434â†’  | Email | `ValidatedEmail` | Constructed via `parse`, not `new` |
   435â†’  | Todo item | `TodoItem` (enum) | `Pending` or `Completed` variant |
   436â†’  | Todo title | `TodoTitle` | Non-empty, max length, validated at construction |
   437â†’
   438â†’### Observability
   439â†’
   440â†’**Structured logging with `tracing`:**
   441â†’- Request ID (UUID) attached to every request via middleware.
   442â†’- Span fields: `request_id`, `method`, `uri`, `status_code`.
   443â†’- Application-level spans for significant operations: `register_user`,
   444â†’  `authenticate`, `create_todo`, etc.
   445â†’- Use `#[tracing::instrument]` for service functions.
   446â†’- Structured fields on errors: what failed, why, relevant IDs.
   447â†’
   448â†’**Log level conventions:**
   449â†’| Level | When to use |
   450â†’|-------|-------------|
   451â†’| `ERROR` | Something is broken, needs human attention |
   452â†’| `WARN` | Unexpected but handled (e.g., invalid login attempt) |
   453â†’| `INFO` | Significant business events (user registered, todo created) |
   454â†’| `DEBUG` | Technical details useful for debugging |
   455â†’| `TRACE` | Very verbose (request/response bodies, SQL queries) |
   456â†’
   457â†’### Database Conventions
   458â†’- **Migrations**: SQLx migrations (`sqlx migrate`), numbered SQL files, version controlled.
   459â†’- **Compile-time checked queries**: `sqlx::query!` and `sqlx::query_as!` macros.
   460â†’- **Offline mode for CI**: `sqlx-data.json` generated via `cargo sqlx prepare`,
   461â†’  committed to version control.
   462â†’- **Connection pool**: `PgPool` shared via Axum state.
   463â†’- **UUIDs for primary keys**: `uuid` type in PostgreSQL, `Uuid` in Rust.
   464â†’- **Timestamps**: `timestamptz` in PostgreSQL, `chrono::DateTime<Utc>` or
   465â†’  `time::OffsetDateTime` in Rust.
   466â†’- **Naming conventions**: snake_case for tables and columns. Plural table names
   467â†’  (`users`, `todos`).
   468â†’
   469â†’### Configuration Management
   470â†’- Layered configuration: `base.yaml` (defaults) -> `local.yaml` (gitignored overrides)
   471â†’  -> environment variables (12-factor style).
   472â†’- **Fail fast**: Missing required configuration at startup panics with a clear message.
   473â†’  No silent defaults for critical settings (database URL, secret keys).
   474â†’- Separate configuration structs: `DatabaseSettings`, `ApplicationSettings`,
   475â†’  `AuthSettings`.
   476â†’
   477â†’### CI/CD Quality Gates
   478â†’
   479â†’Every commit must pass (in order):
   480â†’1. `cargo fmt --check`
   481â†’2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
   482â†’3. `cargo test` (unit + integration)
   483â†’4. `npx playwright test` (browser-based e2e)
   484â†’5. `cargo audit` (dependency vulnerabilities)
   485â†’
   486â†’### Atomic Green Step Pipeline
   487â†’
   488â†’*Every change follows this pipeline. No shortcuts, no skipped steps.*
   489â†’
   490â†’1. **Write code** â€” the smallest meaningful change.
   491â†’2. **`cargo fmt --check`** â€” fix formatting if needed.
   492â†’3. **`cargo clippy -- -D warnings`** â€” fix warnings if needed.
   493â†’4. **`cargo test`** â€” fix failures if needed.
   494â†’5. **`npx playwright test`** â€” fix e2e failures if needed.
   495â†’6. **`git commit`** â€” commit locally. **Do NOT push yet.**
   496â†’7. **All 9 agents review and reach consensus** â€” Driver and Reviewers confirm the change
   497â†’   is correct. If concerns are raised, the Driver fixes them, re-runs steps 2â€“6, and
   498â†’   re-seeks consensus.
   499â†’8. **Once 9/9 consensus â†’ `git push`.**
   500â†’9. **`gh run list --limit 1`** â€” verify CI completes green.
   501â†’10. **Only then begin the next change.**
   502â†’
   503â†’Additional rules:
   504â†’- **Consensus before push.** Code is reviewed locally before it reaches the remote. This
   505â†’  keeps the CI wait out of the feedback loop.
   506â†’- **If CI fails, fix it before writing new code.** A red CI is the team's top priority.
   507â†’  No new features, no new tests, no refactoring until CI is green again.
   508â†’- **One Driver at a time.** Only the designated Driver may modify files. Non-driver agents
   509â†’  MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files. Violation is treated as a
   510â†’  process failure.
   511â†’
   512â†’### Dependency Management
   513â†’
   514â†’Before adding a new crate:
   515â†’- Is it well-maintained? (Recent commits, responsive maintainers)
   516â†’- Is it widely used? (Downloads, reverse dependencies)
   517â†’- Compatible license? (MIT/Apache-2.0 preferred)
   518â†’- Can we avoid it? (Prefer stdlib or existing dependencies)
   519â†’
   520â†’### Documentation
   521â†’- **Code comments**: Only where "why" is not obvious from the code.
   522â†’- **ADRs**: For significant technical decisions. Format: context, decision,
   523â†’  consequences. Stored in `docs/adr/`.
   524â†’- **Domain glossary**: `docs/glossary.md` mapping business terms to code.
   525â†’- **README**: Practical -- how to set up, run tests, run the app.
   526â†’
   527â†’---
   528â†’
   529â†’## 4. Disagreement Resolution
   530â†’
   531â†’### In the Mob (Day-to-Day)
   532â†’1. State the disagreement clearly: "I think X, you think Y."
   533â†’2. Each side explains their reasoning briefly (< 2 minutes each).
   534â†’3. If possible, **try both and let the code decide** -- write a quick test or prototype.
   535â†’4. If one approach is simpler, prefer it (smallest viable decision).
   536â†’5. If still stuck after 5 minutes, the mob votes. Majority wins for day-to-day decisions.
   537â†’   The dissenter can say "I disagree but won't block."
   538â†’
   539â†’### For Significant Decisions (Consensus Protocol)
   540â†’1. Any team member raises the decision point with a proposal and reasoning.
   541â†’2. Each team member shares their perspective (concerns, alternatives, support).
   542â†’3. Discussion continues until:
   543â†’   - **Consensus**: Everyone agrees or consents ("I can live with this").
   544â†’   - **Compromise**: A synthesized solution addresses all concerns.
   545â†’   - **Stand-aside**: "I disagree but won't block" -- recorded and respected.
   546â†’4. If no consensus after **10 rounds** of substantive discussion, **escalate to the
   547â†’   project owner** with:
   548â†’   - Options considered
   549â†’   - Each member's position
   550â†’   - Trade-offs
   551â†’   - Team recommendation (if possible)
   552â†’
   553â†’### When to Involve the Project Owner
   554â†’- Consensus deadlock after 10 rounds
   555â†’- Any proposed change to MVP scope
   556â†’- Questions about target user or business context the team cannot answer
   557â†’- Major architectural pivots that significantly change the timeline
   558â†’- Brief status updates at milestones (keep the owner informed, don't go dark)
   559â†’
   560â†’### Principles
   561â†’- Disagree with the idea, not the person.
   562â†’- Once decided, **disagree and commit** -- no undermining, no "I told you so."
   563â†’- Revisit decisions when new information emerges (from tests, users, or production).
   564â†’- **Write it down**: Major decisions recorded as Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
   565â†’  in `docs/adr/`.
   566â†’
   567â†’---
   568â†’
   569â†’## 5. Architectural Principles
   570â†’
   571â†’1. **Simplicity first**: Choose the simplest approach that could work. Add complexity
   572â†’   only when tests or user feedback demand it. "Could this be simpler?" is always a
   573â†’   valid question.
   574â†’2. **Server-side state**: The server is the source of truth for both data and
   575â†’   presentation. The browser renders what the server sends. No client-side state
   576â†’   management.
   577â†’3. **Pure domain core**: Domain logic is pure functions and types with no I/O. Side
   578â†’   effects live at the boundaries. This makes the domain trivially testable.
   579â†’4. **Make illegal states unrepresentable**: Use the type system to prevent bugs at
   580â†’   compile time. Newtypes, enums, and validated types carry proof of correctness.
   581â†’5. **Production-ready from day one**: Observability, error handling, and structured
   582â†’   logging from the first line of code -- not bolted on later.
   583â†’6. **Accessibility is a quality, not a feature**: Every feature is accessible from the
   584â†’   moment it is built. Inaccessible is broken. ~15-20% of users have some form of
   585â†’   disability -- that is not an edge case.
   586â†’7. **Progressive enhancement**: HTML works -> CSS makes it beautiful -> JS makes it
   587â†’   delightful. Each layer enhances without being required by the layers above.
   588â†’8. **Conventions over cleverness**: Follow established patterns. A login form should
   589â†’   look like every other login form. Don't make the user think. Design for scanning,
   590â†’   not reading. Cut unnecessary words. When in doubt, simplify.
   591â†’9. **Continuous testing**: TDD keeps us honest. The compiler is our first test. We
   592â†’   never skip the red step. Tests read like behavior specifications.
   593â†’10. **Incremental design**: Let the design emerge from tests and feedback. Don't
   594â†’    over-design upfront. Make the code a little better before adding a feature (Tidy
   595â†’    First), but don't redesign the universe. Ship early, learn fast.
   596â†’11. **Walking skeleton first**: Our first milestone is the thinnest possible slice
   597â†’    through the entire stack -- a user can register, log in, see an empty todo list,
   598â†’    add one item, and log out. Everything else builds on top.
   599â†’12. **Validate at the boundary, trust the types**: Input validation happens once, at
   600â†’    the system boundary. It produces typed values that carry proof of validity.
   601â†’    The interior of the system trusts its own types.
   602â†’
   603â†’---
   604â†’
   605â†’## 6. Retrospective Cadence
   606â†’
   607â†’All team members are AI agents operating continuously. Time-based retros (weekly,
   608â†’bi-weekly) do not apply. Instead, retros are triggered by events.
   609â†’
   610â†’- **After each feature**: Retro immediately after completing a feature. What worked?
   611â†’  What didn't? What should we change?
   612â†’- **After significant incidents**: If a build breaks, a major refactor goes sideways,
   613â†’  or the team gets stuck for an extended period, retro after resolution.
   614â†’- **Format**: Simple Start/Stop/Continue.
   615â†’- **Action items**: Every retro produces at most 2-3 concrete action items. These are
   616â†’  tracked and checked at the next retro.
   617â†’- **Agreements are living**: These team agreements can be updated at any retro if the
   618â†’  team reaches consensus.
   619â†’- **Usability testing**: Aim for informal testing -- three users, 20 minutes each --
   620â†’  at natural breakpoints. Even watching one person use the app for 5 minutes reveals
   621â†’  more than a week of discussion.
   622â†’
   623â†’---
   624â†’
   625â†’## 7. Tooling & Repository
   626â†’
   627â†’### Source Control
   628â†’- **Repository**: Public GitHub repository under the `jwilger` account
   629â†’- **License**: MIT
   630â†’- **Branching**: Work on `main` for now. Feature branches if/when the team decides
   631â†’  they are needed.
   632â†’
   633â†’### CI/CD
   634â†’- **GitHub Actions**: CI runs on every push and pull request to `main`.
   635â†’- **Pipeline stages** (in order):
   636â†’  1. `cargo fmt --check`
   637â†’  2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
   638â†’  3. `cargo test`
   639â†’  4. `npx playwright test`
   640â†’- Rust nightly toolchain (matching the project's `rust-toolchain.toml`)
   641â†’- Node.js 22 for Playwright e2e tests
   642â†’- CI must pass before merging any pull request.
   643â†’
   644â†’### Work Tracking
   645â†’- **GitHub Issues**: For tracking features, bugs, and tasks visible to the project owner.
   646â†’- **Claude Task tool**: For in-progress coordination between team members during a session.
   647â†’- Issues and tasks should reference each other when applicable.
   648â†’
   649â†’---
   650â†’
   651â†’## 8. Team Composition Assessment
   652â†’
   653â†’### Current Team (9 members)
   654â†’| Name | Role | Domain |
   655â†’|------|------|--------|
   656â†’| Kent Beck | TDD Coach & Dev Practice Lead | TDD, XP, refactoring |
   657â†’| Scott Wlaschin | Domain Architect | DDD, type-driven design, FP |
   658â†’| Luca Palmieri | Lead Rust Engineer | Axum, SQLx, production Rust |
   659â†’| Carson Gross | Hypermedia Architect & Frontend Lead | HTMX, hypermedia systems |
   660â†’| Lea Verou | Frontend Engineer | CSS, web standards |
   661â†’| Steve Schoger | UI Designer | Visual design, design systems |
   662â†’| Steve Krug | UX Specialist | Usability, user advocacy |
   663â†’| Heydon Pickering | Accessibility Specialist | WCAG, inclusive design |
   664â†’| Marty Cagan | Product Manager | Product discovery, MVP |
   665â†’
   666â†’### Assessment
   667â†’The current team covers all necessary disciplines for this MVP:
   668â†’- **Product**: Marty Cagan
   669â†’- **Design**: Steve Schoger (visual) + Steve Krug (UX) + Heydon Pickering (a11y)
   670â†’- **Architecture**: Scott Wlaschin (domain) + Carson Gross (hypermedia) + Luca
   671â†’  Palmieri (Rust/infra)
   672â†’- **Frontend**: Carson Gross (HTMX) + Lea Verou (CSS/HTML) + Heydon Pickering
   673â†’  (a11y review)
   674â†’- **Backend**: Luca Palmieri + Scott Wlaschin
   675â†’- **Process**: Kent Beck (TDD, mob facilitation)
   676â†’
   677â†’### Potential Gaps to Monitor
   678â†’- **DevOps/Infrastructure**: No dedicated ops person. Luca has deployment experience
   679â†’  but if CI/CD or infrastructure becomes complex, we may want to recruit.
   680â†’- **Security**: Auth is in scope. Luca has experience but if we encounter complex
   681â†’  security requirements, consider a security specialist.
   682â†’- **QA/Testing beyond TDD**: The team practices TDD rigorously, but exploratory
   683â†’  testing and load testing may need attention for production readiness.
   684â†’- **Analytics/Measurement**: If we are serious about "outcomes over output," we will
   685â†’  eventually need to measure whether the product solves the user's problem. Structured
   686â†’  logging (`tracing`) is sufficient for MVP. Analytics instrumentation is a post-MVP gap.
   687â†’
   688â†’**Recommendation**: The current 9-person team is sufficient for the MVP. Revisit at
   689â†’the first retrospective after the core features are built.
   690â†’
   691â†’---
   692â†’
   693â†’## Signatures (Consensus Record)
   694â†’
   695â†’By agreeing to these terms, each team member commits to following these agreements
   696â†’and raising concerns through the established process when they feel the agreements
   697â†’need updating.
   698â†’
   699â†’- [x] Kent Beck
   700â†’- [x] Scott Wlaschin
   701â†’- [x] Luca Palmieri
   702â†’- [x] Carson Gross
   703â†’- [x] Lea Verou
   704â†’- [x] Steve Schoger
   705â†’- [x] Steve Krug
   706â†’- [x] Heydon Pickering
   707â†’- [x] Marty Cagan
   708â†’

# Team Agreements

*Formed: Day 0 -- Team Formation Session*
*Facilitator: Kent Beck*
*Status: APPROVED -- Consensus reached*

## 1. Working Agreements

### Core Principles
- **Outcomes over output**: We measure success by user problems solved, not features
  shipped. Before starting any feature, the team articulates the user problem it solves
  and how we will know it is working (success criteria).
- **MVP discipline is sacred**: Any idea beyond the defined MVP scope gets acknowledged,
  written on the Future List (`docs/future-ideas.md`), and deferred. The answer to
  "wouldn't it be cool if..." is "Yes -- in v2." Scope expansion requires team consensus,
  and Marty will push back hard unless there is a compelling discovery that changes our
  understanding of the user problem.
- **User stories are conversations, not contracts**: User stories guide our work but are
  owned by the whole team. Anyone can propose changes to acceptance criteria with good
  reasoning.

### Communication
- We communicate openly, directly, and respectfully.
- Every team member's perspective is valued equally -- the accessibility specialist's
  concerns carry the same weight as the lead engineer's.
- When raising a concern, propose an alternative. "I don't like X" is less useful than
  "I'd prefer Y because Z."
- We ask questions rather than make assumptions -- especially across disciplines.

### Driver-Reviewer Mob Model
- **Full 9-person mob is always active.** All team members participate in every task.
- **One Driver, eight Reviewers**: The Driver is the sole agent who may modify files
  (using Edit, Write, and Bash tools). All other agents operate as Reviewers -- they
  read code, review changes, and communicate feedback via messages only.
- **Non-driver agents MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files.**
  Violation is treated as a process failure.
- **Driver rotates by task**: The Driver is chosen based on the expertise needed for
  each task. The coordinator designates the Driver when assigning work.
- **Reviewers focus on their specialty**: Each Reviewer contributes from their area of
  expertise (a11y, UX, design, domain modeling, Rust patterns, HTMX, CSS, product,
  TDD discipline) and sends suggestions to the Driver via messages.
- **All suggestions flow through messages**: Reviewers describe intent and concerns,
  not keystrokes. The Driver translates feedback into code changes.
- **Everyone participates**: Designers, PM, accessibility specialist, domain architect --
  every perspective matters. Different eyes catch different things.
- **It's okay to say "I don't understand."** The mob is a learning environment.

### Feature Workflow
Each feature follows this process with the full 9-person mob:

1. **Discovery**: Who is the user? What is the problem? What does "working"
   look like? Identify edge cases (empty state, error state, 1000 items, special
   characters). Marty leads.
2. **Type sketch**: What domain types and states are involved? Quick sketch of
   enums, newtypes, and workflows. Scott leads.
3. **Design intent**: What is the visual hierarchy? What states need designing
   (empty, loading, error, success)? Steve Schoger describes layout, spacing, and
   typography in concrete terms.
4. **TDD cycle**: Red-Green-Refactor. The compiler is our first test. Take the smallest
   step possible. The Driver writes code; Reviewers provide feedback via messages.
   Kent facilitates TDD discipline.
5. **Continuous review**: Reviewers actively review each change as it happens --
   accessibility checked by Heydon, usability checked by Steve Krug, design checked
   by Steve Schoger, hypermedia patterns checked by Carson, domain modeling checked
   by Scott, Rust patterns checked by Luca, product fit checked by Marty.
6. **Consensus gate**: After each atomic green step, all 9 agents confirm the change
   is correct before the Driver proceeds to the next step. The coordinator collects
   consensus before marking any task complete.

### Decision Cadence
- Day-to-day coding decisions (naming, small refactors, local patterns) are made
  naturally in the mob -- no formal process needed.
- Significant decisions (architecture, patterns, library choices, UX patterns, design
  system changes) use the consensus protocol defined in Section 4.

## 2. Definition of Done

A feature is **done** when ALL of the following are true:

### Product
- [ ] The user problem is clearly solved
- [ ] All acceptance criteria from the user story are met
- [ ] A first-time user can accomplish the task without hesitating (Trunk Test)
- [ ] User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature
      being delivered
- [ ] All navigation paths lead somewhere useful (no dead-end pages)
- [ ] Edge cases are handled gracefully (not just the happy path)
- [ ] The feature is deployable -- not just merged, but shippable to real users

### Engineering
- [ ] Tests are written first (TDD: Red-Green-Refactor) and all pass
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test` passes (unit + integration)
- [ ] `npx playwright test` passes -- browser-based e2e tests pass for affected
      user flows
- [ ] Error cases return meaningful HTML responses with structured logging
- [ ] Tracing spans cover the new functionality
- [ ] Database migrations (if any) are committed and `sqlx-data.json` is updated

### Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA)
- [ ] HTML is semantic and uses correct elements (`<button>`, `<label>`, `<ul>`, etc.)
- [ ] All form inputs have visible `<label>` elements (no placeholder-only inputs)
- [ ] All interactive elements are fully keyboard operable
- [ ] Screen reader testing (VoiceOver, NVDA, or equivalent) confirms: interactive
      elements are announced correctly, state changes are communicated, and dynamic
      content updates are announced via live regions
- [ ] ARIA used only when native HTML semantics are insufficient (with comments explaining why)
- [ ] Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI)
- [ ] Color is never the only indicator of state (always a secondary visual cue)
- [ ] Focus management is correct after HTMX swaps (see HTMX Focus Protocol below)
- [ ] Custom `:focus-visible` styles are present with sufficient contrast
- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` is respected for all animations/transitions

### Design
- [ ] Visual design follows the established design token system
- [ ] All states are designed: empty, loading, error, success
- [ ] Visual hierarchy is clear -- one primary focal point per screen
- [ ] Spacing is consistent and uses the defined scale

### Frontend
- [ ] Core functionality works via standard HTML forms and links (progressive
      enhancement -- HTMX and TypeScript enhance but are not required for basic operation)
- [ ] HTMX attributes follow locality of behavior (self-describing elements)
- [ ] TypeScript (if any) is non-critical and has accessible alternatives

### Process
- [ ] The mob has continuously reviewed code, design, usability, and accessibility
      during development
- [ ] All 9 team members have reviewed and consent to the completed work
- [ ] Labels and copy follow the Copy Standards (see Section 3)

## 3. Code Conventions and Patterns

### Project Structure

```
src/
  main.rs              -- Startup, configuration loading, server binding
  lib.rs               -- Re-exports for integration test access
  configuration.rs     -- Config structs, environment-aware loading
  startup.rs           -- Application builder (Router, DB pool, middleware)
  routes/              -- HTTP handlers (thin: extract, delegate, respond)
    mod.rs
    health_check.rs
  domain/              -- Pure domain types and logic (NO I/O)
    mod.rs
  services/            -- Application services (orchestrate domain + infra)
    mod.rs
  infrastructure/      -- Database queries, external service clients
    mod.rs
  templates/           -- Askama HTML templates
tests/
  api/                 -- Integration tests against the full HTTP stack
    mod.rs
    helpers.rs         -- TestApp, spawn_app(), shared fixtures
    health_check.rs
```

Dependencies flow inward: routes -> services -> domain + infrastructure.
Domain depends on nothing. This makes the domain layer trivially testable.

### Rust Conventions

**Type-driven domain modeling:**
- Newtypes for all domain identifiers and constrained values: `UserId(Uuid)`,
  `Email(String)`, `Title(String)`, `TodoItemId(Uuid)`
- Enums for state machines -- make illegal states unrepresentable:
  ```rust
  // GOOD: the type system prevents invalid states
  enum TodoItem {
      Pending { title: Title, created_at: DateTime<Utc> },
      Completed { title: Title, created_at: DateTime<Utc>, completed_at: DateTime<Utc> },
  }
  ```
- **Parse, don't validate**: Instead of `fn validate(input: &str) -> bool`, write
  `fn parse(input: String) -> Result<Email, ValidationError>`. Validation that returns
  a boolean throws away information. Parsing produces a typed value that carries the
  proof of validity. Validate once at the boundary; never re-validate inside the system.

**Workflows as type-safe pipelines:**
```
UnvalidatedInput -> ValidatedInput -> DomainEvent -> PersistenceResult
```
Each step's output type is the next step's input. You cannot skip validation or
persist before validating.

**Error handling (three layers):**
- **Domain errors**: Custom enum with `thiserror`. Domain terms only -- no HTTP concepts,
  no infrastructure types. A `TodoError::TitleEmpty` must never contain a `sqlx::Error`.
  Domain errors and infrastructure errors are separate types, translated at the boundary.
  ```rust
  #[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
  pub enum TodoError {
      #[error("Todo title cannot be empty")]
      TitleEmpty,
      #[error("Todo title too long (max {max}, got {actual})")]
      TitleTooLong { max: usize, actual: usize },
      #[error("Todo not found")]
      NotFound { id: TodoItemId },
      #[error(transparent)]
      Unexpected(#[from] anyhow::Error),
  }
  ```
- **Service errors**: Map domain errors to actionable responses. Add context.
- **Route errors**: Implement `IntoResponse` for error types. Return meaningful HTML
  (not raw 500s). Every error path produces a user-facing response.
- **Always add context** when propagating errors. A bare `?` that loses context is a
  code smell.
- **Log at error boundaries**: Every error crossing an architectural boundary gets
  logged with structured `tracing` fields.

**Idiomatic Rust:**
- Prefer iterators over loops, pattern matching over conditionals
- `?` operator for error propagation with context
- `#[must_use]` on important return types
- Compile-time guarantees: SQLx `query!`/`query_as!` macros, Askama templates

**Testing strategy (TDD):**
- **Unit tests** (`#[cfg(test)] mod tests`): Pure domain logic, validation, type
  conversions. Fast, no I/O, no mocking. **Litmus test: if you need a mock to test
  domain logic, I/O has leaked into the domain layer. Push it back to the boundary.**
- **Integration tests** (`tests/` directory): Full HTTP stack with real database.
  `TestApp` struct with `spawn_app()` helper. Unique database per test run
  (random name, run migrations, drop after).
- **No mocks for the database**: Use real PostgreSQL via Docker. Mocks give false
  confidence.
- **Test names as behavior specifications**:
  ```rust
  #[tokio::test]
  async fn registration_returns_422_when_email_is_missing() { ... }
  ```
- **Acceptance tests as usability statements**: "When I type a todo and press Enter,
  it appears in my list." Tests describe what the user expects to happen.
- **Property-based tests** with `proptest` where appropriate.
- **End-to-end tests** (`e2e/` directory): Playwright browser tests against the
  running application. Cover critical user journeys (register, login, core feature,
  logout). Use role-based and label-based locators (`getByRole`, `getByLabel`) so
  tests double as accessibility assertions. Keep this layer thin -- only add e2e
  tests for flows that integration tests cannot adequately verify.
- **Accessibility testing** (four layers):
  - *Automated*: axe-core (or equivalent) in the test suite for missing labels,
    contrast violations, and structural issues
  - *Keyboard*: Manual keyboard navigation testing for every interactive flow
  - *Screen reader*: Manual VoiceOver/NVDA testing for every feature
  - *Structural*: HTML validation for semantic correctness
  Tests can assert on roles, labels, and ARIA attributes -- documenting the expected
  screen reader experience as living specifications.

### HTML / HTMX Conventions

**The Hypermedia Contract:**
- **Return HTML, not JSON.** Every UI endpoint returns HTML. No JSON API layer.
- **Semantic HTML first**: `<button>` for actions, `<a>` for navigation, `<form>` for
  data submission, `<ul>`/`<ol>` for lists. Never `<div onclick>`.
- **Form accessibility**: Every input has a visible `<label>` (never placeholder-only).
  Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby` and announced
  via `aria-live="polite"` regions. Use `aria-live="assertive"` only for critical errors.
- **Locality of behavior**: Every HTMX-enhanced element is self-describing. You
  understand what it does by looking at it alone.
- **No client-side state**: The DOM is the state, the server is the source of truth.
  No client-side data stores. The only acceptable client-side state is transient UI
  state for progressive enhancement (e.g., drag position).

**Server endpoint conventions:**
- **Full page requests** (no `HX-Request` header): Return complete HTML page with
  layout, nav, landmarks.
- **HTMX requests** (with `HX-Request` header): Return just the HTML fragment for
  the swap.
- **`HX-Trigger` response header** for cross-component updates (e.g., updating a
  todo count after deletion).
- **Same URL, same handler**: One handler checks the `HX-Request` header to decide
  whether to return a full page or a fragment. No separate `/api/` routes.
- **HTTP methods used semantically**: GET = read, POST = create, PUT/PATCH = update,
  DELETE = delete.
- **Status codes**: 200 for success with HTML, 422 for validation errors with error
  HTML, 200 with empty body for deletion (allows HTMX swap to remove element).

**Progressive enhancement (non-negotiable):**
- The app MUST work without JavaScript. Forms submit, links navigate, content is
  readable with HTML alone.
- HTMX enhances the experience (partial updates, no full page reloads).
- TypeScript is only for things neither HTML nor HTMX can do.

**HTMX Focus Management Protocol:**
After every HTMX swap:
- If an element was **removed**: Move focus to the logical next element.
- If content was **updated in place**: Keep focus where it was.
- Use `aria-live="polite"` regions to announce state changes to screen readers.
- Test every HTMX interaction with Tab key before considering it done.

**Page structure (every page):**
- Skip link to main content (first focusable element)
- Landmark regions: `<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`, `<footer>`
- Logical heading hierarchy: one `<h1>` per page, no skipped levels
- Descriptive `<title>` element

### CSS Conventions

**Progressive enhancement layers:**
1. **Layer 1 (HTML)**: Functional and readable with zero CSS.
2. **Layer 2 (CSS)**: Beautiful -- layout, typography, color, transitions, focus styles.
3. **Layer 3 (TypeScript)**: Delightful -- drag-and-drop, advanced keyboard shortcuts.

**CSS organization with cascade layers:**
```css
@layer reset, tokens, base, layout, components, utilities;
```
- **reset**: Normalize/reset styles
- **tokens**: Custom properties (design tokens)
- **base**: Element-level styles (body, headings, links, forms)
- **layout**: Page-level layout (CSS Grid)
- **components**: Component-specific styles (todo-item, auth-form)
- **utilities**: Helper classes (visually-hidden, etc.)
- No `!important` unless in the utilities layer for intentional overrides.
- CSS nesting is fine for component-scoped styles but avoid nesting deeper than
  2 levels.

**Design tokens (CSS custom properties on `:root`):**
- **Spacing scale** (4px base): 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64, 96 -- encoded as
  `--space-xs` through `--space-3xl`
- **Color palette**: oklch color space for perceptual uniformity. One primary color,
  neutral gray palette (50-900), semantic colors (danger, success, warning). Most UI
  is grayscale; color is for emphasis and interaction.
- **Typography**: System font stack (`system-ui, sans-serif`). Type scale from xs to
  3xl with defined line-height. Tighter for headings, looser for body.
- **Shadows**: Three levels (sm, md, lg). Never combine shadows and borders on the
  same element.
- **Border radius**: Three sizes (sm, md, lg).
- **All values reference tokens -- no hardcoded hex values or pixel values in
  component styles.**

**Design tokens are a prerequisite**: The token system (spacing, colors, typography,
shadows, radii) must be established and agreed upon before any feature UI work begins.
This is foundational -- without it, inconsistent one-off values accumulate from day one.

**Design token governance**: Adding a new color, spacing value, or typography level
goes through the mob (Steve Schoger approves design token changes).

**Design token flow**: Steve Schoger defines the visual design -> Lea encodes as CSS
custom properties -> Components reference only tokens -> Themes/dark mode reassign
properties. No build step. No JSON-to-CSS pipeline.

**Modern CSS features:**
- `:has()` for parent-based styling (e.g., `li:has(input:checked)` for completed todos)
- Container queries where components need to respond to container size
- Logical properties (`margin-inline`, `padding-block`) for future RTL support
- `clamp()` for fluid typography
- View Transitions API for smooth HTMX swap animations
- `@supports` for graceful degradation of newer features

**Accessibility in CSS:**
- Custom `:focus-visible` styles with at least 3:1 contrast. Never `outline: none`
  without a replacement.
- `prefers-reduced-motion`: All animations/transitions wrapped in a motion query.
  Reduced or no motion as the alternative.
- `prefers-color-scheme`: Supported if/when the team decides to add dark mode.
- Color is never the only state indicator.

**Responsive design**: Mobile-first. Default styles target the smallest viewport;
enhancements added via `min-width` media queries. Breakpoints: mobile (default),
desktop (~1024px). A todo list should work beautifully on a phone.

**Browser targets**: Last 2 versions of evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari,
Edge). No IE11. `@supports` for graceful degradation. No polyfills unless necessary.

### TypeScript Conventions
- **No framework**: Vanilla TypeScript with DOM APIs.
- **Progressive enhancement pattern**: Feature works without JS, TypeScript enhances.
- **Event delegation**: Listeners on parent containers, not individual elements.
- **State via data attributes**: TypeScript toggles `data-*` attributes or classes;
  CSS handles all visual changes based on those.
- **No CSS-in-JS**: Styles live in CSS files. TypeScript never sets inline styles
  (except computed values like drag position).
- **Thin layer**: If a TypeScript file is getting large, we are probably doing
  something CSS or HTMX could handle.

### Copy Standards
- **Labels**: 1-2 words when possible.
- **Error messages**: Plain language. Say what went wrong, suggest what to do next.
  "That email isn't registered -- want to create an account?" not "Error: User not found."
- **Button labels**: Describe the action ("Add todo", "Sign in"), not vague labels
  ("Submit", "OK").
- **No happy talk**: Every word earns its place or gets cut. No introductory paragraphs
  that say nothing useful.
- **Design for scanning, not reading**: Users don't read pages, they scan. Clear
  headings, short text, prominent calls to action, no walls of text.
- **Cut the text, then cut it again**: If you wrote a paragraph, ask "can I say this
  in half the words?"

### Domain Language and Modeling Conventions
- Code uses the ubiquitous language of the domain. "Complete a task" ->
  `complete_task()`, not `update_status()`.
- Domain types are explicit -- no primitive obsession. Every domain concept gets its
  own type, even if it is "just a string" or "just a UUID" underneath.
- Newtypes are constructed only through validated factory methods (the `parse`
  pattern). You cannot create a `TodoTitle` from an arbitrary string without going
  through validation.
- Every `is_*` boolean flag is a candidate for an enum. Ask: "what states does this
  represent?" and model them explicitly.
- Maintain a domain glossary in `docs/glossary.md` mapping business terms to types:

  | Domain Term | Rust Type | Notes |
  |-------------|-----------|-------|
  | User | `User` | Authenticated user with valid credentials |
  | Email | `ValidatedEmail` | Constructed via `parse`, not `new` |
  | Todo item | `TodoItem` (enum) | `Pending` or `Completed` variant |
  | Todo title | `TodoTitle` | Non-empty, max length, validated at construction |

### Observability

**Structured logging with `tracing`:**
- Request ID (UUID) attached to every request via middleware.
- Span fields: `request_id`, `method`, `uri`, `status_code`.
- Application-level spans for significant operations: `register_user`,
  `authenticate`, `create_todo`, etc.
- Use `#[tracing::instrument]` for service functions.
- Structured fields on errors: what failed, why, relevant IDs.

**Log level conventions:**
| Level | When to use |
|-------|-------------|
| `ERROR` | Something is broken, needs human attention |
| `WARN` | Unexpected but handled (e.g., invalid login attempt) |
| `INFO` | Significant business events (user registered, todo created) |
| `DEBUG` | Technical details useful for debugging |
| `TRACE` | Very verbose (request/response bodies, SQL queries) |

### Database Conventions
- **Migrations**: SQLx migrations (`sqlx migrate`), numbered SQL files, version controlled.
- **Compile-time checked queries**: `sqlx::query!` and `sqlx::query_as!` macros.
- **Offline mode for CI**: `sqlx-data.json` generated via `cargo sqlx prepare`,
  committed to version control.
- **Connection pool**: `PgPool` shared via Axum state.
- **UUIDs for primary keys**: `uuid` type in PostgreSQL, `Uuid` in Rust.
- **Timestamps**: `timestamptz` in PostgreSQL, `chrono::DateTime<Utc>` or
  `time::OffsetDateTime` in Rust.
- **Naming conventions**: snake_case for tables and columns. Plural table names
  (`users`, `todos`).

### Configuration Management
- Layered configuration: `base.yaml` (defaults) -> `local.yaml` (gitignored overrides)
  -> environment variables (12-factor style).
- **Fail fast**: Missing required configuration at startup panics with a clear message.
  No silent defaults for critical settings (database URL, secret keys).
- Separate configuration structs: `DatabaseSettings`, `ApplicationSettings`,
  `AuthSettings`.

### CI/CD Quality Gates

Every commit must pass (in order):
1. `cargo fmt --check`
2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. `cargo test` (unit + integration)
4. `npx playwright test` (browser-based e2e)
5. `cargo audit` (dependency vulnerabilities)

### Atomic Green Step Pipeline

*Every change follows this pipeline. No shortcuts, no skipped steps.*

1. **Write code** â€” the smallest meaningful change.
2. **`cargo fmt --check`** â€” fix formatting if needed.
3. **`cargo clippy -- -D warnings`** â€” fix warnings if needed.
4. **`cargo test`** â€” fix failures if needed.
5. **`npx playwright test`** â€” fix e2e failures if needed.
6. **`git commit`** â€” commit locally. **Do NOT push yet.**
7. **All 9 agents review and reach consensus** â€” Driver and Reviewers confirm the change
   is correct. If concerns are raised, the Driver fixes them, re-runs steps 2â€“6, and
   re-seeks consensus.
8. **Once 9/9 consensus â†’ `git push`.**
9. **`gh run list --limit 1`** â€” verify CI completes green.
10. **Only then begin the next change.**

Additional rules:
- **Consensus before push.** Code is reviewed locally before it reaches the remote. This
  keeps the CI wait out of the feedback loop.
- **If CI fails, fix it before writing new code.** A red CI is the team's top priority.
  No new features, no new tests, no refactoring until CI is green again.
- **One Driver at a time.** Only the designated Driver may modify files. Non-driver agents
  MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files. Violation is treated as a
  process failure.

### Dependency Management

Before adding a new crate:
- Is it well-maintained? (Recent commits, responsive maintainers)
- Is it widely used? (Downloads, reverse dependencies)
- Compatible license? (MIT/Apache-2.0 preferred)
- Can we avoid it? (Prefer stdlib or existing dependencies)

### Documentation
- **Code comments**: Only where "why" is not obvious from the code.
- **ADRs**: For significant technical decisions. Format: context, decision,
  consequences. Stored in `docs/adr/`.
- **Domain glossary**: `docs/glossary.md` mapping business terms to code.
- **README**: Practical -- how to set up, run tests, run the app.

## 4. Disagreement Resolution

### In the Mob (Day-to-Day)
1. State the disagreement clearly: "I think X, you think Y."
2. Each side explains their reasoning briefly (< 2 minutes each).
3. If possible, **try both and let the code decide** -- write a quick test or prototype.
4. If one approach is simpler, prefer it (smallest viable decision).
5. If still stuck after 5 minutes, the mob votes. Majority wins for day-to-day decisions.
   The dissenter can say "I disagree but won't block."

### For Significant Decisions (Consensus Protocol)
1. Any team member raises the decision point with a proposal and reasoning.
2. Each team member shares their perspective (concerns, alternatives, support).
3. Discussion continues until:
   - **Consensus**: Everyone agrees or consents ("I can live with this").
   - **Compromise**: A synthesized solution addresses all concerns.
   - **Stand-aside**: "I disagree but won't block" -- recorded and respected.
4. If no consensus after **10 rounds** of substantive discussion, **escalate to the
   project owner** with:
   - Options considered
   - Each member's position
   - Trade-offs
   - Team recommendation (if possible)

### When to Involve the Project Owner
- Consensus deadlock after 10 rounds
- Any proposed change to MVP scope
- Questions about target user or business context the team cannot answer
- Major architectural pivots that significantly change the timeline
- Brief status updates at milestones (keep the owner informed, don't go dark)

### Principles
- Disagree with the idea, not the person.
- Once decided, **disagree and commit** -- no undermining, no "I told you so."
- Revisit decisions when new information emerges (from tests, users, or production).
- **Write it down**: Major decisions recorded as Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
  in `docs/adr/`.

## 5. Architectural Principles

1. **Simplicity first**: Choose the simplest approach that could work. Add complexity
   only when tests or user feedback demand it. "Could this be simpler?" is always a
   valid question.
2. **Server-side state**: The server is the source of truth for both data and
   presentation. The browser renders what the server sends. No client-side state
   management.
3. **Pure domain core**: Domain logic is pure functions and types with no I/O. Side
   effects live at the boundaries. This makes the domain trivially testable.
4. **Make illegal states unrepresentable**: Use the type system to prevent bugs at
   compile time. Newtypes, enums, and validated types carry proof of correctness.
5. **Production-ready from day one**: Observability, error handling, and structured
   logging from the first line of code -- not bolted on later.
6. **Accessibility is a quality, not a feature**: Every feature is accessible from the
   moment it is built. Inaccessible is broken. ~15-20% of users have some form of
   disability -- that is not an edge case.
7. **Progressive enhancement**: HTML works -> CSS makes it beautiful -> JS makes it
   delightful. Each layer enhances without being required by the layers above.
8. **Conventions over cleverness**: Follow established patterns. A login form should
   look like every other login form. Don't make the user think. Design for scanning,
   not reading. Cut unnecessary words. When in doubt, simplify.
9. **Continuous testing**: TDD keeps us honest. The compiler is our first test. We
   never skip the red step. Tests read like behavior specifications.
10. **Incremental design**: Let the design emerge from tests and feedback. Don't
    over-design upfront. Make the code a little better before adding a feature (Tidy
    First), but don't redesign the universe. Ship early, learn fast.
11. **Walking skeleton first**: Our first milestone is the thinnest possible slice
    through the entire stack -- a user can register, log in, see an empty todo list,
    add one item, and log out. Everything else builds on top.
12. **Validate at the boundary, trust the types**: Input validation happens once, at
    the system boundary. It produces typed values that carry proof of validity.
    The interior of the system trusts its own types.

---

## 6. Retrospective Cadence

All team members are AI agents operating continuously. Time-based retros (weekly,
bi-weekly) do not apply. Instead, retros are triggered by events.

- **After each feature**: Retro immediately after completing a feature. What worked?
  What didn't? What should we change?
- **After significant incidents**: If a build breaks, a major refactor goes sideways,
  or the team gets stuck for an extended period, retro after resolution.
- **Format**: Simple Start/Stop/Continue.
- **Action items**: Every retro produces at most 2-3 concrete action items. These are
  tracked and checked at the next retro.
- **Agreements are living**: These team agreements can be updated at any retro if the
  team reaches consensus.
- **Usability testing**: Aim for informal testing -- three users, 20 minutes each --
  at natural breakpoints. Even watching one person use the app for 5 minutes reveals
  more than a week of discussion.

---

## 7. Tooling & Repository

### Source Control
- **Repository**: Public GitHub repository under the `jwilger` account
- **License**: MIT
- **Branching**: Work on `main` for now. Feature branches if/when the team decides
  they are needed.

### CI/CD
- **GitHub Actions**: CI runs on every push and pull request to `main`.
- **Pipeline stages** (in order):
  1. `cargo fmt --check`
  2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
  3. `cargo test`
  4. `npx playwright test`
- Rust nightly toolchain (matching the project's `rust-toolchain.toml`)
- Node.js 22 for Playwright e2e tests
- CI must pass before merging any pull request.

### Work Tracking
- **GitHub Issues**: For tracking features, bugs, and tasks visible to the project owner.
- **Claude Task tool**: For in-progress coordination between team members during a session.
- Issues and tasks should reference each other when applicable.

---

## 8. Team Composition Assessment

### Current Team (9 members)
| Name | Role | Domain |
|------|------|--------|
| Kent Beck | TDD Coach & Dev Practice Lead | TDD, XP, refactoring |
| Scott Wlaschin | Domain Architect | DDD, type-driven design, FP |
| Luca Palmieri | Lead Rust Engineer | Axum, SQLx, production Rust |
| Carson Gross | Hypermedia Architect & Frontend Lead | HTMX, hypermedia systems |
| Lea Verou | Frontend Engineer | CSS, web standards |
| Steve Schoger | UI Designer | Visual design, design systems |
| Steve Krug | UX Specialist | Usability, user advocacy |
| Heydon Pickering | Accessibility Specialist | WCAG, inclusive design |
| Marty Cagan | Product Manager | Product discovery, MVP |

### Assessment
The current team covers all necessary disciplines for this MVP:
- **Product**: Marty Cagan
- **Design**: Steve Schoger (visual) + Steve Krug (UX) + Heydon Pickering (a11y)
- **Architecture**: Scott Wlaschin (domain) + Carson Gross (hypermedia) + Luca
  Palmieri (Rust/infra)
- **Frontend**: Carson Gross (HTMX) + Lea Verou (CSS/HTML) + Heydon Pickering
  (a11y review)
- **Backend**: Luca Palmieri + Scott Wlaschin
- **Process**: Kent Beck (TDD, mob facilitation)

### Potential Gaps to Monitor
- **DevOps/Infrastructure**: No dedicated ops person. Luca has deployment experience
  but if CI/CD or infrastructure becomes complex, we may want to recruit.
- **Security**: Auth is in scope. Luca has experience but if we encounter complex
  security requirements, consider a security specialist.
- **QA/Testing beyond TDD**: The team practices TDD rigorously, but exploratory
  testing and load testing may need attention for production readiness.
- **Analytics/Measurement**: If we are serious about "outcomes over output," we will
  eventually need to measure whether the product solves the user's problem. Structured
  logging (`tracing`) is sufficient for MVP. Analytics instrumentation is a post-MVP gap.

**Recommendation**: The current 9-person team is sufficient for the MVP. Revisit at
the first retrospective after the core features are built.

content.text:
I now have a thorough understanding of the codebase and the two bugs being addressed. Let me analyze what I see:

**Bug 1 - Welcome page nav links:** The `index.html` template has both `{% block nav %}` links (Create account, Sign in) at the top AND the hero CTAs below with the same links. The nav links are redundant for sighted users since the hero CTAs are the primary focal point.

**Bug 2 - Invisible checkbox characters:** The `todo_item.html` uses Unicode characters `☑` (checked) and `☐` (unchecked) inside a `<button>` with class `todo-item__toggle`. Looking at the CSS, the `.todo-item__toggle` sets `background: none` and removes the border, BUT the base button styles (lines 370-391) set `color: white` and `background-color: var(--color-primary)`. The component styles override `background: none`, but the `color: white` from the base layer persists because the component layer doesn't explicitly set a text color for `.todo-item__toggle`. The cascade layers mean the component layer wins, but only for properties it declares -- `color` is inherited from the base button style which is `white`.

Wait, actually cascade layers work differently. The `components` layer has higher priority than `base`. So the `.todo-item__toggle` rules in `components` would override base button rules. But `.todo-item__toggle` only sets `background: none`, `border: none`, `padding`, `cursor`, `font-size`, `line-height`, `flex-shrink`. It does NOT reset `color` -- so `color: white` from the base button style still applies because the component selector doesn't declare a `color` property.

Actually, let me reconsider. Layer priority means `components` layer rules win over `base` layer rules for the **same property on matching selectors**. The base layer sets `color: white` on `button`. The components layer sets various properties on `.todo-item__toggle` but NOT `color`. Since there's no competing `color` declaration in the components layer, the `color: white` from the base layer still applies to buttons. The layer order only matters when there's a conflict on the same property -- here there is no conflict, so both apply.

This means the checkbox characters (Unicode text) inherit `color: white` on a `background: none` (transparent) button, making them invisible against the white/light gray page background.

I'm now ready to provide design feedback when the Driver (Lea Verou) requests my review. I'll wait for her message.
